title: About Russell Johnston
text: Hello, I am Russell Johnston a current student persuing a degrees in Sports Media and Broadcasting at the University of Nebraska. I Have been privelaged enough to be able to coach high school football at a local school. In coaching football, you learn how much numbers are involved in the game, from yards to inches, and most importantly statistics after the fact. However, numbers dont automatically give you what your looking for until you dive deeper or look at the bigger picture.

title: The Inconsistant Airraid of the Cardiac Bears.
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(nflfastR)
library(ggplot2)
library(gt)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)

pbp2025<-load_pbp(2025)

bearspass<-pbp2025 |>
  filter(posteam == "CHI",
  play_type =="pass"
  )|>
  arrange(desc(yards_gained))|>
select(posteam,week,down,yards_gained,yards_after_catch,air_yards,shotgun,score_differential,season_type,side_of_field,pass_length,pass_location,pass_attempt,pass_touchdown,game_date,pass_attempt, game_half,complete_pass,incomplete_pass,qtr)


The Chicago bears 2025 passing game showed inconsistiency throughout the game. However, Caleb Williams showed consistency of throwing the ball to the outside parts of the feild with a heavy favorite to the right side of the field.


Code
bearstable <- bearspass |>
  group_by(week) |>
  summarize(
    attempts = n(),
    completions = sum(complete_pass, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    completion_pct = completions / attempts
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(completion_pct))

bearstable |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    week = "Week",
    attempts = "Passing Attempts",
    completions = "Completions",
    completion_pct = "Completion Percentage"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Chicago Bears Completion Percentage by Week",   
    subtitle = "Completion percentage fluctuated throughout the season without a clear upward trend."  
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: NFL play-by-play data via nflverse" 
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Credit: Russell Johnston" 
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black",size = "x-large", weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_title(groups = "title")    
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(size = "medium"),
    locations = cells_title(groups = "subtitle")  
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(size = "small"),
    locations = cells_source_notes()              
  )








Chicago Bears Completion Percentage by Week


Completion percentage fluctuated throughout the season without a clear upward trend.

Taking a dive into completion percentages. Before the bye week Caleb stayed consistant with a surge in week 3 recording his highest completion percentage of the year. Williams percentage swung over 20% from the worst game in week 13 and his best game of week 3. Week-to-week passing attempts and completions fluctated without an obvious trend.


Code
ggplot(bearspass, aes(x = week)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = pass_attempt), fill = "navy") +
  geom_col(aes(y = complete_pass), fill = "orange") +
  labs(
    title = "Attempts vs Completions Each Week",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Completed vs Attempts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()+
labs(
    title = "Chicago Bears Passing Efficiency Never Stabilized",
    subtitle = "Completion percentage fluctuated week to week without a clear upward trend",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Completion Percentage",
    caption = "Source: NFL play-by-play data via nflverse | Chart: Rusty Johnston"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, hjust = 0)
  )









The Chicago Bears passing game never showed consitency throughout the season. Throught the weeks completions make up a steady perportion of attempts, but the gap is still promanate.Prior to the bye week in week 5, the bears were consitant in the first two games with a slump in the third, and a rise in the 4th game. Later in the season we see a surge in attempts but we dont see the same surge in completed passes. Therefore, as time goes on the Bears performance seems to reset every week. This indicates the Bears relied on quantity and chance rather than acuuracy and execution. Overall, this chart shows a passing game that struggled to find a consistant identity.


Code
bearswaf <-data.frame( category = c("Middle", "Right", "Left"),
  count = c(150, 307, 205))

rows <- 20

waffle_bears <- bearswaf |>
  uncount(count) |>
  mutate(
    x = (row_number() - 1) %/% rows + 1,
    y = (row_number() - 1) %% rows + 1
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_tile(
    data=waffle_bears, 
    aes(
      x = x, y = y, fill = category
      ), 
    color = "white", 
    linewidth = .5) +
  coord_equal() + 
  theme_void() +
labs(
    x = "1 square = 1 pass",
    title="Caleb Williams Favored Throwing the Ball to the Right Side of the feild.", 
    subtitle="Every completed pass location ",
    caption="Source: NFLverse | Graphic by Russell Johnston",
    fill = "Record") + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    strip.text.x = element_text(size = 6, hjust=0)
  )









The Bears have favored to throw the ball to the right side of the field. Caleb Williams threw 307 balls to the right 205 balls to the left and 150 balls in thhe middle of the field. The Bears overwelmingly favored the right side. This imbalance is not something an offensive cordinator wants to see because it can be easily countered with a defensive cordinator who finds out this tendency.


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = bearspass,
    aes(
      x = factor(week),
      y = yards_gained,
      color = pass_length
    ),
    alpha = 0.6,
    position = position_jitter(width = 0.4)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ qtr, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Chicago Bears Passing Yards by Week and Quarter",
    subtitle = "Each dot represents a pass play. Colors indicate pass length.",
    x = "Week of the Season",
    y = "Yards Gained on the Play",
    color = "Pass Length",
    caption = "Source: NFL play-by-play data via nflverse\nCredit: Rusty Johnston"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),   
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),               
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),                 
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11),                   
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9)                  
  )









There are a couple of trends we can see. This graph shows the pass length of each quarter per week. We can see the bears struggled in the 2nd quarter with throwing long passes. If we look at qaurter 4 we can see a surge in shorter passses as we got later into the season. This graph shows a 5th qaurter for the two games the Bears played in overtime going 1 and 1. In week 16 the Bears threw two passes in overtime to win against an absoulute poverty of a franchise the Green Bay Packers. although most of the fourth quarter passes were short. The Bears saw the most distance throughout the season in the 4th quarter. This chart also highlights the inconsitency of deep balls througout the season. While there was an occasional deep ball the Bears clearly focused on short yardage and the idea of marching down the feild. The spread of data in the 4th qaurter reflects the Bears were either in a close game or losing. The 2025 Bears were given the nickname “cardiac Bears” when they came back and won 7 of their 12 victories while trailing with two minutes left in the fourth. Overall the data paints a clear picture of a lack of balance and effiency. We can see effeciency in the fourth quarter but there was no consitant trend of growth game to game. Overall, the data shows the lack of balance and efficiency. For the Bears to get past the NFC North Championship, they need to execute more throughout the entire game, change up passing locations, and improve completion throughout the season.

title: Waverly Football Offensive on Paper
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(ggplot2)

state<- read_csv("data/state.csv")




Code
yards <- state |> 
  select("PLAY TYPE","GN/LS") 




Code
library(tidyverse)

yards <- state |> 
  mutate(
    play_type = case_when(
      !is.na(PASSER_Name) ~ "Passing",
      !is.na(RUSHER_Name) ~ "Rushing",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |> 
  filter(!is.na(play_type)) |> 
  group_by(play_type) |> 
  summarize(total_yards = sum(`GN/LS`, na.rm = TRUE))




Code
ggplot(yards) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = play_type, y = total_yards, fill = play_type),
           stat = "identity") +
  labs(
    title = "Passing vs Rushing Yards ",
    subtitle = "Total offensive production by play type in the state championship",
    x = "Play Type",
    y = "Total Yards",
    caption = "Source: State game data | Credit: Russell Johnston"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









During the State Championship we relied heavily on the run game. The main reason is simple. They could not stop are running back. The wildest part about this is Gretna East stacked the box with 8 guys leaving the secondary in a cover zero and yet could not stop the run game.


Code
eff<-read_csv("eff.csv") 




Code
eff <- read_csv("eff.csv", skip = 1)

eff_clean <- eff |>
  rename(
    down = 1,
    efficient = 2,
    count = 3,
    percent = 4
  ) |>
  fill(down) |>
  filter(down != "ALL Down EFF")

eff_clean |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    down = "Down",
    efficient = "Efficient?",
    count = "Count",
    percent = "Percent"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Efficiency by Down",
    subtitle = "How often the offense stayed efficient on each down"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: eff.csv | Credit:Russell Johnston"
  )

Source: eff.csv | Credit:Russell Johnston








This table shows the effciency on each down throughout the season. Our efficiency is decided by yards gained. First down we need to get 4 yards on 2nd down we need to have gained 3 yards to be eficent on third down we need to convert. The data does back this up having the least amount of efficiency in third down. If we look at 4th down we see we were the most succesful. But, why? Our offensive ideology is 4 downs to get ten yards. Most programs force the 3rd down conversion and immediatly punt no matter what. For us, situations do play a factor but if it is 4th and short and we are not pinned back. We were going to take the chance and try to convert.


Code
week2 <-read_csv("Beatrice.csv")




Code
week5 <-read_csv("Standing Bear .csv")




Code
week4 <-read_csv("Elkhorn High.csv")




Code
week1 <-read_csv("bennigton.csv")




Code
week6 <-read_csv("Beatrice.csv")




Code
week7 <-read_csv("platt.csv")




Code
week8 <-read_csv("norris.csv")




Code
week9 <-read_csv("pius.csv")




Code
week10<-read_csv("northwest.csv")




Code
week11 <-read_csv("sewerdrd2.csv")




Code
week12<-read_csv("benrd2.csv")




Code
week3<-read_csv("mcook.csv")




Code
alldata<- bind_rows(week1,week2,week3,week4,week5,week6,week7,week8,week9,week10,week11,week12,state)




Code
week1 <- week1 |> mutate(week = 1)
week2 <- week2 |> mutate(week = 2)
week3 <- week3 |> mutate(week = 3)
week4 <- week4 |> mutate(week = 4)
week5 <- week5 |> mutate(week = 5)
week6 <- week6 |> mutate(week = 6)
week7 <- week7 |> mutate(week = 7)
week8 <- week8 |> mutate(week = 8)
week9 <- week9 |> mutate(week = 9)
week10 <- week10 |> mutate(week = 10)
week11 <- week11 |> mutate(week = 11)
week12 <- week12 |> mutate(week = 12)
state <- state |> mutate(week = 13)




Code
alldata<- bind_rows(week1,week2,week3,week4,week5,week6,week7,week8,week9,week10,week11,week12,state)




Code
weekly <- alldata |>
  mutate(
    type = case_when(
      !is.na(PASSER_Name) ~ "Passing",
      !is.na(RUSHER_Name) ~ "Rushing"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(type)) |>
  group_by(week, type) |>
  summarize(yards = sum(`GN/LS`, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")




Code
ggplot(weekly) +
  geom_line(aes(x = week, y = yards, color = type), size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Passing vs Rushing Yards by Week",
    subtitle = "Tracking offensive production over the season",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Total Yards",
    color = "Play Type",
    caption = "Source: Team data | Credit: Russell Johnston"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









This chart tracks the offensive production throughout the season. We see a heavy dominance in the run game early in the season and in the post season. in the middle of the season we see a shift and a more balanced pass and run game. When we look at weeks 8 and 9, are run game was managed by the defense very well. However we see a peak in the passing game in week 8. In week 8 we saw Norris. This game went into overtime and was won off a game winning field goal. This shows Norris defense prioritized stopping our star running back Axemann and not defending the secondary. As we head into playoffs we see a dramatic spike in rushing yards showing the domination of Axemann. Overall our offense never had a consistent identity. Going from run heavy to balanced and back to run heavy. When one team found how to slow down Axemann our Quarterback Brockston Teply was able to find the wholes in the secondary when teams would stack the box. Through yard production overtime we can identify the main reason we were able to have an undefeated season. As Mrs. Tuohy once said “RUN THE DANG BALL”
